topic: An employee discovers the company is being sold
raw_output:
[story 1]

Eleanor stared at the glossy presentation slides projected onto the conference room wall. The words—"Strategic Acquisition," "Synergistic Growth"—felt like a physical assault. She gripped the edge of the mahogany table so tightly her knuckles bleached white. For six years, she had poured her soul into this company, building the very infrastructure she now saw being packaged and shipped off to some faceless corporate entity. A slow, hot flush crept up her neck, settling in her cheeks. "Are we really doing this?" she managed, her voice surprisingly steady, though a tremor vibrated deep in her chest. The silence that followed was heavy, thick with unspoken betrayal. She pushed her chair back, the scrape of metal against the floor sounding deafening in the quiet room.

[story 2]

Mark reread the internal memo for the fifth time, his jaw tight enough to crack a tooth. The language used to describe the impending sale—'optimization,' 'streamlining redundancies'—was a deliberate, sterile mockery of everything he had built here. He felt a sudden, cold knot tighten beneath his sternum, radiating outward. He picked up his pen, intending to sign off on the quarterly report, but his hand shook too violently. He slammed the pen down, the sound sharp and disproportionate to the situation. "This is unacceptable," he muttered, the words tasting like ash. He stood up abruptly, needing space, needing to physically move away from the suffocating atmosphere of the boardroom.

[story 3]

From her desk, Sarah watched the CEO’s smug smile as he concluded the announcement. A strange, hollow sensation settled in her stomach, like water draining from a punctured vessel. She didn't speak immediately. Instead, she began meticulously organizing the files on her desktop, her movements precise, almost frantic. Every folder, every project file, felt suddenly tainted, a monument to a chapter that was being violently ripped out. When her manager approached, offering a reassuring pat on the shoulder, Sarah flinched subtly, pulling her posture inward. "I just need to review my transition documents," she said, her tone clipped and devoid of warmth. The way she avoided eye contact spoke volumes about the profound disappointment she was trying desperately to conceal.
